title: Valley-Controlled Viscosity of Two-Dimensional Dirac Fluids
abstract: Motivated by recent experiments in weakly hybridized small-angle twisted bilayer graphene, we investigate how valley imbalance affects the viscosity of two-dimensional Dirac fluids. We show that shifting the two low-energy Dirac cones relative to one another provides a direct knob to control the viscosity of the electron fluid. As the splitting is increased, the system passes through distinct transport regimes associated with valley depletion, charge-neutrality crossover, and the onset of electron-hole scattering, producing a pronounced nonmonotonic response. To place this result in context, we also analyze the viscosity in monolayer graphene (MLG) and two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG). We show that, due to the strong dependence of its inertial mass density on temperature, the kinematic viscosity of MLG is a monotonically decreasing function of temperature. Our results identify valley control as a route to tuning hydrodynamic transport in Dirac materials and clarify the interplay between band structure, scattering phase space, and screening in setting the viscous response.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the viscosity of 2D Dirac fluids can be controlled via valley imbalance, showing a unique nonmonotonic response to energy splitting. By comparing different band structures, the authors clarify how the temperature dependence of inertial mass density distinguishes Dirac fluids from conventional 2D electron gases.
main_problem: The paper investigates how valley imbalance (an energy offset between Dirac cones) affects the viscosity of two-dimensional Dirac fluids, specifically in systems like small-angle twisted bilayer graphene.
main_result: The viscosity exhibits a strongly nonmonotonic dependence on valley splitting, featuring a maximum when a valley approaches charge neutrality and a minimum when electron-hole scattering is enabled. In contrast, monolayer graphene shows monotonic kinematic viscosity, and 2DEG shows a minimum in both shear and kinematic viscosity.
method: The authors use a linearized semiclassical Boltzmann approach focusing on momentum-conserving electron-electron scattering with dynamically screened Coulomb interactions.
model_or_system: The primary model is an effective two-cone Dirac system with an energy offset Delta between valleys, motivated by small-angle twisted bilayer graphene. Comparison models include monolayer graphene (MLG) and a parabolic-band 2D electron gas (2DEG).
key_observables: Shear viscosity (eta), kinematic viscosity (nu), viscosity transport time (tau_v), Drude weight (D), and nonlocal resistance profiles.
important_parameters: Valley splitting (Delta), temperature (T), carrier density (n), chemical potential (mu), and the crossover between degenerate Fermi-liquid and nondegenerate Fermi-gas regimes.
main_assumptions: Assumes no intervalley scattering, incompressible flow, and that Umklapp processes, phonons, and static disorder are subdominant. It also assumes the separation between valley centers is large enough to neglect inter-valley wavefunction overlap.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the valley-polarized two-cone problem; Figure 2 illustrates the two-body scattering process; Figure 5(a) plots MLG shear viscosity vs temperature; Figure 8 shows shear viscosity vs valley splitting; Figure 9 compares numerical and analytical estimates for the local minimum line.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of valley-imbalanced Dirac fluids, develops a kinetic framework using the Boltzmann equation, analyzes the nonmonotonic viscosity response in the two-cone model, provides comparative studies for MLG and 2DEG, and includes an appendix with detailed mathematical derivations of the relaxation rate.
why_interesting: The study of hydrodynamic transport and viscosity in Dirac fluids provides deep insights into many-body dynamics and the interplay between band structure and scattering phase space, which is relevant to understanding non-equilibrium transport in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic realization of the valley-polarized two-cone problem studied in this work. The two low-energy Dirac cones are offset by an energy splitting ∆. This models the weak valley-dependent energy shift expected when the layers are weakly hybridized at small twist angle and a displacement field is applied. The grey plane marks zero energy in our calculation.

FIG. 2. Representative two-body scattering process entering the collision integral. The four quasiparticle states are la- belled by their momenta and band/valley indices. The wiggly line represents the (dynamically screened) Coulomb interac- tion, with q the transferred momentum.
Figure 2 shows a representative momentum-conserving process contributing to the collision integral.

FIG. 3. Kinematic viscosity of the valley-imbalanced SA- TBG two-cone Dirac model as a function of the energy offset ∆, at n0 = 1011 cm−2 and for various temperatures. At low and intermediate temperatures the finite-∆maximum and the local minimum are both clearly resolved. For T > Tc, the global maximum remains at ∆= 0, but the profile around the origin becomes broad, which makes the numerical extrac- tion of a distinct finite-∆peak increasingly noisy.

FIG. 4. (a) Temperature dependence of the maximum of the kinematic viscosity, ∆max(T) = arg max∆[ν(∆, T)]. The blue dots are the numerically extracted maxima, the red solid line is the low-temperature analytic estimate of Eq. (23), and the black dashed line is a phenomenological fit of the form ∆⋆p
